title: Losing the War of the Future
gist: This is a paywall-truncated excerpt of Paul Scharre's essay (full argument not visible in the collected text); the visible portion reports that in the U.S. campaign against Iran, U.S. air dominance and over 13,000 strikes did not stop Iran from inflicting real losses — over 2,200 missiles and 4,400 drones launched, at least eight U.S. aircraft destroyed or damaged, multiple radars hit, and seven U.S. service members killed.

themes.theme: Air dominance did not prevent U.S. losses

insights.claim: During the U.S. campaign against Iran, the U.S. military achieved air dominance and conducted over 13,000 strikes on Iranian targets using traditional airpower.
insights.speaker: Paul Scharre
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline of overwhelming U.S. conventional air superiority against which the following losses are measured.
insights.quote: The U.S. military pounded Iranian targets, conducting over 13,000 strikes.
insights.locator: In its recent campaign against Iran

insights.claim: Despite total U.S. air dominance, over the 39-day conflict (February 28-April 8, 2026) Iran launched over 2,200 missiles and 4,400 drones against countries in the region, destroying or damaging at least eight U.S. aircraft, hitting multiple U.S. radars, and killing seven U.S. service members.
insights.speaker: Paul Scharre
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that mass missile/drone salvos can inflict meaningful losses on U.S. forces even under complete U.S. air superiority — supports investment theses in counter-drone and layered missile defense, and flags exposure risk for traditional crewed-aircraft/radar assets in future high-intensity conflicts.
insights.quote: Iran launched over 2,200 missiles and 4,400 drones against countries in the region.
insights.locator: Over the course of the 39-day conflict

top_entities: Center for a New American Security
